lead: Build Management with Maven + Claude Code
sub: One config file to rule them all

body.code: # One file? Easy. javac Calculator.java # 200 files + 30 libraries? javac -cp lib/gson-2.10.jar:lib/junit-5.9.jar:lib/mockito-4.11.jar: lib/... src/main/java/com/example/*.java

items: Classpath management becomes impossible
Library versions conflict
“Works on my machine, not on yours”
No standard way to run tests, package JARs, generate docs

title: What Is Maven?

rows: Write a shell script per OS

items: Build management: compile, test, package — one command, any machine
Dependency management: declare what you need, Maven downloads it from Maven Central
Convention over configuration: standard directory layout means zero setup

body.code: # One command does everything: mvn clean install What happens behind the scenes: 1. Deletes old build output (clean) 2. Downloads missing dependencies (resolve) 3. Compiles all .java files (compile) 4. Runs all unit tests (test) 5. Packages into .jar (package) 6. Installs to local repository (install)
body.note: Without Maven, each of these steps would be a separate script you maintain yourself.

body.quote: Maven is not exciting. That is the point — builds should be boring and predictable.

desc: The tool that answers: how do I build this project on any machine?

body.code: my-project/ pom.xml ← project configuration src/ main/ java/ com/example/ Calculator.java ← production code resources/ application.properties ← config files test/ java/ com/example/ CalculatorTest.java ← test code target/ ← build output (NEVER commit!)
body.note: This structure is a convention. Maven expects it. Don’t fight it.

title: The pom.xml: Heart of Every Maven Project
pom.xml as a Capability Contract
desc: A capability contract is a formal declaration: “here is what I am, what I need, and what I can do.”

items: Reproducibility — anyone can build the project with mvn install, no setup guide needed
Transparency — every dependency and plugin is declared, nothing is hidden
Automation — CI servers (Jenkins) read pom.xml and know exactly what to do
Version control — pom.xml is committed to Git, so build config has full history

body.quote: If it is not in the pom.xml, it does not exist. No more “works on my machine” — the contract guarantees it works everywhere.

title: POM Structure: The Four Sections
desc: Every pom.xml is organized into four logical areas.

items: <groupId>, <artifactId>, <version> — unique coordinate (GAV)
<packaging> — output type: jar, war, or pom (parent only)
<parent>, <modules> — inheritance and multi-module structure
<properties> — variables reused across the POM (Java version, encoding, library versions)
<dependencies>, <dependencyManagement> — libraries your project needs
<name>, <url>, <description> — project metadata
<developers>, <contributors> — team members
<licenses> — project license declarations
<build> — directories, extensions, resources, plugins
<reporting> — site generation and report plugins
<issueManagement>, <ciManagement>, <scm> — environment info
<repositories>, <distributionManagement> — where to download / upload artifacts
<profiles> — conditional build configurations (release, coverage)

title: Maven Build Lifecycle: Complete 23 Phases
desc: Bold = phases you call directly. Grey = run automatically by plugins.

body.code: 1 validate Check project is correct & all info available 2 initialize Set up build state (properties, directories) 3 generate-sources Generate source code (e.g. from WSDL/XSD) 4 process-sources Filter/process source code 5 generate-resources Generate resources for packaging 6 process-resources Copy resources to target/classes 7 compile Compile src/main/java → target/classes 8 process-classes Post-process .class files (e.g. bytecode enhancement) 9 generate-test-sources Generate test source code 10 process-test-sources Filter/process test source code 11 generate-test-resources Generate test resources 12 process-test-resources Copy test resources to target/test-classes 13 test-compile Compile src/test/java → target/test-classes 14 process-test-classes Post-process test .class files 15 test Run unit tests (fails → build stops) 16 prepare-package Final preparation before packaging 17 package Create .jar or .war in target/ 18 pre-integration-test Set up integration test environment 19 integration-test Run integration tests 20 post-integration-test Tear down integration test environment 21 verify Run quality checks (CheckStyle, PMD, SpotBugs) 22 install Copy .jar to local ~/.m2/ repository 23 deploy Upload to remote repository (Nexus) Each phase runs ALL phases before it. mvn package = phases 1–17 | mvn install = phases 1–22

body.card: The commands you will actually use:
items: mvn compile — does it build? (phases 1–7)
mvn test — do tests pass? (phases 1–15)
mvn package — create the deliverable (phases 1–17)
mvn clean package — fresh build from scratch
mvn clean install — build + put in local repo (phases 1–22)
mvn clean deploy — build + publish to Nexus (phases 1–23)

title: Maven Plugins: Who Does the Actual Work
desc: Maven itself is just a framework. Plugins execute each lifecycle phase.

rows: Calls javac with correct classpath and Java version
Finds and runs *Test.java classes with JUnit
Assembles .class files into a JAR archive

body.quote: You configure plugins in pom.xml. Maven calls them at the right lifecycle phase. You never run plugins manually.

title: Dependencies: Maven Downloads Libraries for You

items: Maven reads pom.xml
Checks local cache (~/.m2/repository/)
Downloads missing JARs from Maven Central
Adds them to classpath automatically

body.code:
ERROR: class found in two modules: 
  gson-2.8.9.jar and gson-2.10.1.jar

items: Library A needs Gson 2.8, Library B needs Gson 2.10. Both on your classpath.

body.code: # Your best friend for debugging: mvn dependency:tree
body.note: This is Maven’s hardest problem — and where AI helps most.

title: AI-Augmented: Claude Code Explains Maven Errors

body.code: [ERROR] Failed to execute goal org.apache.maven.plugins:maven-compiler-plugin:3.11.0:compile on project calculator: Compilation failure: [ERROR] /src/main/java/Calculator.java:[15,32] incompatible types: possible lossy conversion from double to int
body.note: Maven errors are verbose. The actual problem is on the last line.

body.code: You: My Maven build fails. Here is the error. [paste above] Claude Code: Line 15 of Calculator.java assigns a double to an int variable. Java won’t do this automatically because you’d lose the decimal part. Fix: change the variable type from int to double, or cast explicitly: (int) result
body.note: Give AI the exact error message. Don’t say “my build fails” — that’s asking it to guess. Paste the error — that’s grounding it in facts.

body.code: You: I need to parse JSON files in my Calculator project. What library should I use? Claude Code: For a simple project, use Gson (Google). It’s lightweight, has no transitive dependencies, and is the most common choice for small Java projects. I’ll add it to your pom.xml: <dependency> <groupId>com.google.code.gson</groupId> <artifactId>gson</artifactId> <version>2.11.0</version> </dependency> Verify: mvn dependency:tree | grep gson
body.note: Always verify the version Claude Code suggests. Check search.maven.org or mvnrepository.com — AI may suggest outdated versions.

body.quote: Trust but verify. AI picks reasonable defaults — but “reasonable” is not the same as “current.”

title: Read Tools vs. Write Tools
desc: Classifying operations by risk level — a universal engineering pattern.

items: Read = safe to run anytime — nothing changes, nothing breaks
Write (local) = changes your machine — reversible, but check what you overwrite
Write (external) = changes shared systems — cannot undo, needs extra caution

body.quote: This pattern appears everywhere in engineering: read is free, local write is careful, external write needs approval.

items: Create a new empty directory
Create the Maven directory structure by hand (no Claude Code!)
Write a minimal pom.xml by hand
Create a simple App.java in the correct directory
Run mvn compile — does it work?
If it fails, fix it yourself first, then use Claude Code to explain the error

body.quote: Goal: Understand the structure before letting AI generate it.

items: Your Calculator needs to read math expressions from a JSON file
Ask Claude Code: “Add a JSON parsing library to my Maven project”
Verify the version is current (check mvnrepository.com)
Create a class that reads {"operation": "add", "a": 5, "b": 3}
Run mvn compile — does it resolve?
Run mvn dependency:tree — how many total dependencies?

body.quote: Discussion: Which commands would you auto-approve? Which need caution?

items: Maven = build management + dependency management
Standard structure: src/main/java, src/test/java, pom.xml
Lifecycle: clean → compile → test → package → install → deploy
Dependencies are downloaded automatically from Maven Central
Never commit target/, passwords, API keys, or secrets to Git
Claude Code augments Maven: explains errors, manages dependencies, fixes pom.xml
Industry: pom.xml = capability contract; Maven commands = read/write tools with risk levels
Always verify AI-suggested dependency versions
